flashcards.back: If n items are placed into m containers and n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item.

flashcards.front: What is the generalized pigeonhole principle?
flashcards.back: If n items are placed into m containers, then at least one container contains ⌈n/m⌉ items.

flashcards.front: In a group of 367 people, how many must share a birthday? Why?
flashcards.back: At least 2 people share a birthday, because there are only 366 possible days (including Feb 29), so 367 > 366 guarantees a match.

flashcards.front: What is the minimum number of socks to pick to guarantee a matching pair in darkness?
flashcards.back: 3 socks. With 2 colors (pigeonholes), picking 3 socks ensures at least 2 are the same color.

flashcards.front: How many people needed in a room to guarantee 3 share a birth month?
flashcards.back: At least 25 people. With 12 months, using ⌈n/12⌉ ≥ 3 gives n ≥ 25.

flashcards.front: State the strong form of pigeonhole principle
flashcards.back: If n items are distributed into k groups, then at least one group contains at least ⌈n/k⌉ items or at most ⌊n/k⌋ items.

flashcards.front: In any group of 6 people, what is guaranteed about mutual acquaintances or strangers?
flashcards.back: At least 3 are mutual acquaintances OR at least 3 are mutual strangers (Ramsey number R(3,3)=6).

flashcards.front: How many integers from 1 to 100 must be chosen to guarantee two with sum 101?
flashcards.back: 52 integers. The pairs (1,100), (2,99), ..., (50,51) form 50 pairs; picking 51 guarantees one complete pair.

flashcards.front: What is the Dirichlet drawer principle?
flashcards.back: Another name for the pigeonhole principle, attributed to German mathematician Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet.

flashcards.front: What is the minimum number of cards from a deck to guarantee 13 cards of same suit?
flashcards.back: 40 cards. With 4 suits, ⌈n/4⌉ ≥ 13 gives n ≥ 40 (worst case: 12 of each suit = 48, one more ensures 13th).

flashcards.front: Prove at least 2 people in Kolkata have same number of hairs on their head
flashcards.back: Maximum hairs on human head ≈ 200,000. Kolkata population > 200,000, so by pigeonhole, at least 2 share same hair count.

flashcards.front: How many students needed in a class to guarantee 2 with same first letter of name?
flashcards.back: 27 students. 26 letters in alphabet, so 26+1=27 guarantees repetition.

flashcards.front: What is the infinite pigeonhole principle?
flashcards.back: If infinite set is partitioned into finite number of sets, at least one set is infinite. Used in Ramsey theory and topology.

flashcards.front: What is the Erdős–Szekeres theorem related to pigeonhole?
flashcards.back: Any sequence of mn+1 distinct numbers contains an increasing or decreasing subsequence of length m+1.

flashcards.front: How many books on shelf to guarantee 3 consecutive of same subject?
flashcards.back: If there are k subjects, need (3-1)×k+1 = 2k+1 books. With 3 subjects: 7 books guarantees 3 consecutive of one subject.
